arguments.old_string:
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
arguments.new_string:
/** * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format. * *

In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of a single record * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.

* *

Thread Safety: Implementations of this interface are not thread-safe. External * synchronization is required for concurrent access.

* *

Role in Kafka Protocol: Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.

* *

Performance Characteristics: For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible * from the backing buffer without additional copying.

* * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections * @see Record Individual record in a batch */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

arguments.old_string:
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
arguments.new_string:
/** * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format. * *

In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of a single record * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.

* *

Thread Safety: Implementations of this interface are not thread-safe. External * synchronization is required for concurrent access.

* *

Role in Kafka Protocol: Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.

* *

Performance Characteristics: For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible * from the backing buffer without additional copying.

* * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections * @see Record Individual record in a batch */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

/** * Check whether the CRC-32C checksum of this batch is correct. * *

The checksum covers the batch header (from attributes byte onwards) and all record data. * The partition leader epoch field is excluded from CRC computation to avoid recomputation * when the field is assigned by the broker.

* * @return true if the computed checksum matches the stored checksum, false otherwise * @see #checksum() to retrieve the stored checksum value */ boolean isValid(); /** * Raise an exception if the checksum of this batch is not valid. * * @throws CorruptRecordException if the stored checksum does not match the computed checksum * @see #isValid() for non-exceptional validation */ void ensureValid();

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will
     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will
     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return
     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the
     * behavior is equivalent.
     *
     * Because this requires deep iteration for older magic versions, this method should be used with
     * caution. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for all magic versions.
     *
     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record
     *         as described above).
     */
    long baseOffset();
arguments.new_string:
/** * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. * *

For magic version prior to 2, this returns the offset of the first record in the batch, * which generally requires deep iteration. For magic version 2 and above, this returns the * first offset of the original record batch (prior to compaction), which is stored directly in * the batch header and is efficiently accessible. For non-compacted topics, the behavior is * equivalent across versions.

* *

Performance Note: For older magic versions (v0, v1), this method requires iterating * through records and should be used with caution. {@link #lastOffset()} is safer and more * efficient for all magic versions.

* * @return the base offset of this record batch * @see #lastOffset() for the more efficiently accessible last offset */ long baseOffset();

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return The closeable iterator
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
arguments.new_string:
/** * Return a streaming iterator that delays decompression of the record stream until records are * actually requested via {@link Iterator#next()}. * *

If the message format does not support streaming iteration (e.g., legacy formats), the * normal iterator is returned instead. This method enables memory-efficient processing of * compressed batches by decompressing records on-demand rather than upfront.

* *

Performance Characteristics: For compressed batches, this avoids allocating large * decompression buffers upfront. For small record batches, the cost of allocating a buffer * (e.g., 64 KB for LZ4) can dominate decompression cost. Using a {@code decompressionBufferSupplier} * that reuses buffers across multiple batches can significantly reduce allocation overhead.

* * @param decompressionBufferSupplier supplier of ByteBuffer(s) for decompression. Using a * reusable buffer supplier has significant performance impact for small batches. * {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING} creates a new buffer per decompression. * * @return a closeable iterator over records in this batch. The caller must close this iterator * to release decompression resources. * * @see BufferSupplier for buffer management strategies */ CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);

arguments.old_string:
/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for
 * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and
 * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.
 *
 * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty
 * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches
 * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId
 * is expired from lack of activity.
 *
 * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp
 * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the
 * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp
 * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.
 *
 * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type
 * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set
 * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains
 * the previous value prior to becoming empty.
 *
 * The current attributes are given below:
 *
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 */
public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Concrete implementation of {@link RecordBatch} for magic version 2 and above. * *

Binary Format: Records in magic v2+ follow the schema below: * 

 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32 (CRC-32C from attributes to end of batch)
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64 (timestamp of first record, or delete horizon if flag set)
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64 (or -1 if not set)
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
* When compression is enabled, compressed record data follows directly after the count field.

* *

Thread Safety: This class is not thread-safe. Batch state can be modified in-place * (e.g., via {@link #setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType, long)}) and must be externally synchronized for * concurrent access.

* *

CRC Computation: The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial covers all bytes from the attributes * field through the end of the batch. The partition leader epoch field is excluded to avoid CRC * recomputation when assigned by the broker. The magic byte must be parsed before interpreting bytes * between batch length and CRC.

* *

Log Compaction Semantics: Magic v2+ preserves the first and last offset/sequence numbers * from the original batch during log cleaning. This ensures producer state can be correctly restored * after leader failover. Base sequence is needed for duplicate detection. Empty batches may be retained * to preserve producer state until a new sequence is written or the producer ID expires.

* *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Header fields (offsets, timestamps, producer ID) are efficiently accessible directly from the * buffer without iteration.
    • *
  • For compressed batches, records are decompressed on-demand via iterators.
    • *
  • Record count is efficiently accessible via {@link #countOrNull()}.
    • *

* * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for creating/managing batches in memory * @see RecordBatch base interface */ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {

/** * Gets the base timestamp of the batch, which is used to calculate individual record timestamps * from their timestamp deltas. * *

The base timestamp semantics depend on context: *

    *
  • For normal batches, this is the timestamp of the first record.
    • *
  • For empty batches (after compaction), this remains the timestamp of the original first * record.
    • *
  • If the delete horizon flag is set, this represents the time at which tombstone records * and aborted transaction markers should be removed.
    • *

* *

Individual record timestamps are computed as: {@code baseTimestamp + timestampDelta}

* * @return the base timestamp in milliseconds since epoch, or -1 ({@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP}) * for empty batches without a delete horizon * * @see #maxTimestamp() for the batch's maximum timestamp * @see #deleteHorizonMs() to check if delete horizon flag is set */ public long baseTimestamp() { return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET); }

/** * In-memory implementation of {@link Records} backed by a ByteBuffer. * *

Primary Use Cases: *

    *
  • Reading or modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches from the log or network.
    • *
  • Iterating over batches and records for filtering, validation, or processing.
    • *
* To create new records from scratch, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or factory methods like * {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)}, {@link #withIdempotentRecords}, etc.

* *

Thread Safety: This class is not thread-safe. The backing ByteBuffer and batch * objects can be modified in-place (e.g., CRC updates during log appending). External synchronization * is required for concurrent access. However, individual batch iteration is safe if the buffer contents * are not modified during iteration.

* *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Batch iteration is lazy—batches are parsed incrementally, not all at once.
    • *
  • Record decompression is lazy—only decompressed when actually read via iterators.
    • *
  • No copying of record data; all reads directly reference the backing buffer via slices.
    • *
  • {@link #validBytes()} requires scanning all batches; the result is cached.
    • *

* *

Buffer Management: The ByteBuffer backing this instance can be in several states: *

    *
  • Read-only: Created via {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)}, position/limit managed externally.
    • *
  • Writable: Created via {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder#build()}, position and limit reflect valid data.
    • *

* * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for creating new record batches * @see RecordBatch for batch-level operations * @see Record for record-level operations */ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
        return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier);
    }
arguments.new_string:
/** * Filter records based on retention rules, writing results to a destination buffer. * *

This method is used for log compaction and record expiration, applying batch and record-level * retention decisions via the provided {@code filter}. Batches can be entirely deleted or retained * (possibly with records removed). Empty batches may be retained to preserve producer state for * idempotent/transactional producers.

* *

Filtering Process: *

    *
  1. Each batch is evaluated via {@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)}.
    2. *
  2. If batch retention is {@link RecordFilter.BatchRetention#DELETE}, the entire batch is skipped.
    3. *
  3. Otherwise, each record is evaluated via {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)}.
    4. *
  4. Retained records are written to {@code destinationBuffer}, possibly in a new batch if * modification was necessary.
    5. *

* *

Performance: For compressed batches, this method decompresses records on-demand using * the supplied {@code decompressionBufferSupplier}. Reusable buffer suppliers significantly improve * performance for small batches where allocation dominates. Buffer overflow is handled transparently * by allocating larger buffers as needed.

* * @param filter filter defining batch and record retention logic * @param destinationBuffer buffer to write filtered records to. If overflow occurs, a larger buffer * is allocated and returned in {@code FilterResult#outputBuffer()} * @param decompressionBufferSupplier supplier for decompression buffers. For performance with small * batches, use {@link BufferSupplier#create()} which reuses buffers within the lifetime * of the returned resource. * * @return a {@link FilterResult} containing the filtered records buffer, metrics (messages read/retained, * bytes read/retained), and max offset/timestamp information * * @see RecordFilter for batch and record retention semantics * @see FilterResult for the structure of returned filter metrics */ public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) { return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier); }

/** * Create a {@code MemoryRecords} instance for reading existing record batches from a buffer. * *

The returned instance provides read-only access to record batches. The buffer is read from * its current position to its limit. For batch iteration, the buffer position is not modified; * a duplicate buffer is used internally to preserve the original state.

* *

Use Cases: Reading records from the log, network buffer, or in-memory batch collections. * Use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or {@link #withRecords} variants to create new records.

* * @param buffer ByteBuffer containing serialized record batches. The buffer's position and limit * define the readable range. The buffer must remain valid for the lifetime of the returned * MemoryRecords instance. * * @return a MemoryRecords instance wrapping the buffer for reading batches * * @throws NullPointerException if buffer is null * * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder to create new records */ public static MemoryRecords readableRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) { return new MemoryRecords(buffer); }

/** * Create a builder for constructing new record batches using the current record format version. * *

This convenience method uses {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2). For legacy * format support, use {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)}.

* *

The builder initializes batches with: *

    *
  • No producer ID (default non-idempotent producer)
    • *
  • No transaction markers
    • *
  • Current system time for {@code LOG_APPEND_TIME} timestamp type
    • *

* * @param buffer ByteBuffer to write batches to. Must have sufficient remaining capacity for * the records to be added. Can be passed to subsequent {@code builder()} calls to * accumulate multiple batches. * @param compression compression type for the records * @param timestampType whether timestamps are CREATE_TIME (client) or LOG_APPEND_TIME (broker) * @param baseOffset offset of the first record in the batch * * @return a MemoryRecordsBuilder ready to append records * * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder#append for adding records to the batch * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder#build for finalizing the batch */ public static MemoryRecordsBuilder builder(ByteBuffer buffer, Compression compression, TimestampType timestampType, long baseOffset) { return builder(buffer, RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, timestampType, baseOffset); }

/** * Create a {@code MemoryRecords} instance from the provided records using default settings. * *

This is a convenience factory for creating record batches with: *

    *
  • Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (v2)
    • *
  • Base Offset: 0
    • *
  • Timestamp Type: CREATE_TIME
    • *
  • No producer ID (non-idempotent)
    • *
  • No transactions
    • *

* *

Typical Use: Creating test records or simple non-idempotent producer batches.

* * @param compression the compression type (NONE, SNAPPY, LZ4, ZSTD) * @param records the records to include in the batch * * @return a MemoryRecords instance containing a single batch with the provided records * * @throws IllegalStateException if records cannot fit in a single batch due to buffer size constraints * * @see #withIdempotentRecords for idempotent producer records * @see #withTransactionalRecords for transactional records */ public static MemoryRecords withRecords(Compression compression, SimpleRecord... records) { return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, records); }
